content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

October 23, 1981

William P. Hopkins
City Attorney
City of Anaheim
Civic Center, Third Floor
200 South Anaheim Blvd.
Anaheim, CA 92805

Re: Your Request for Written
Advice

Dear Mr. Hopkins:

Thank you for your recent letter seeking the advice of
the Fair Political Practices Commission.

FACTS

Based upon your letter of October 15, 1981, and our
various telephone conversations, the available material
facts are as follows:

1. The Decision

The City Council of the City of Anaheim consists of
four councilmembers and the mayor. At its meeting on Tuesday,
October 27, 1981, the City Council will consider the adoption
of a proposed ordinance establishing an "Occupancy Inspection
Program," modeled on a program currently in effect in the
City of Pasadena. You have provided me with a copy of the
Pasadena Ordinance and the Pasadena program, the latter
states, inter alia:

The purpose of the Occupancy Inspection Program is to
have residential structures inspected for safety and
code compliance . . . each time a change of occupancy
occurs.

The ordinance, by its own terms applies to all residential
structures, whether rented or owner occupied. It is triggered

William P. Hopkins
October 23, 1981
Page Two


by a change in occupants or by a sale or other transfer of
interest.

     No person shall occupy, change the use of or sell,
exchange, rent, lease or otherwise permit any unit
which is hereafter vacated by the occupant thereof to
be reoccupied until a certificate of occupancy . . .
is issued....

     Before a certificate of occupancy may be issued an
inspection must be performed by city officials to determine
if the structure is habitable, in compliance with applicable
health and safety codes. If the structure is found to be in
compliance then the certificate is issued and is valid for
12 months or until the new occupants vacate, whichever comes
later. If the structure is found not to be in compliance,
then a notice of needed corrections is issued and these must
be made prior to occupation, sale, etc. A fee of approximately
$30 is charged by the City of Pasadena for its efforts. You
have indicated that Anaheim may need to charge as much as
$45, if such an ordinance is adopted.

2. The Public Officials

     (a) Mayor John Seymour: Mayor Seymour is a real estate
broker. Mayor Seymour owns three single-family houses, and
one condominium which he rents out. Mayor Seymour estimates
the aggregate value of these properties to be $500,000; all
are located in the City of Anaheim. In his business as a
real estate broker, Mayor Seymour has earned approximately
$70,000 in commissions in the last 12 months for managing
numerous residential rental units in the City of Anaheim;
including single-family dwellings, apartments and condominiums.
Mayor Seymour is in the process of selling 80 percent of his
interest in his brokerage, retaining a 20 percent interest.
Mayor Seymour owns his home.

     (b) Councilmember Donald Roth: Councilmember Roth is
a real estate broker in an "association" with another broker,
Galen Compton. They share profits 50-50. The brokerage
firm engages in property management of residential rental
properties, managing seven units in the City of Anaheim.
During the last 12 months, the firm has earned approximately
$2,800-3,000 in commissions for this property management
activity. Councilmember Roth owns no residential rental

William P. Hopkins
October 23, 1981
Page Three


units within the City of Anaheim, he does own his home.
Councilmember Roth feels that the proposed ordinance would
have a financial effect on his property management business.

(c) Councilmember E. Llewellyn Overholt, Jr.: Councilmember
Overholt is an attorney in private practice as a sole practitioner.
During the past 12 months he has done legal work for clients
who own residential rental property and for clients who are
real estate firms. His practice is concentrated in the
field of probate law. The extent of the residential rental
real estate interests of these clients is unknown as is the
amount of legal fees received by Councilmember Overholt from
any of these clients. Councilmember Overholt owns his home.

ANALYSIS

Government Code Section 87100-1/ requires a public official
to refrain from participation in any "governmental decision
in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest." Clearly, Mayor Seymour and Councilmembers Roth
and Overholt are public officials and the decision in question
would be made in their governing capacity and, therefore, is
governmental.

We must then examine whether or not the requisite financial
interest is present in the case of the pending decision.

(a) Mayor John Seymour: The real estate industry in
Anaheim opposes the proposed ordinance because it feels that
the occupancy inspection program will cost residential rental
property owners and managers money due to the payment of
fees, delays in occupancy and the cost of repairs. Mayor
Seymour has a substantial income ($70,000) from rental property
management. Obviously, he has numerous clients who are
sources of income to him in excess of $250 in the last 12
months. These persons have interests in real property which
will be financially affected by the adoption of the ordinance.
This financial effect would appear to be significant2/ since
the real estate industry is strongly opposed to the proposal.

1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code.

2/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a).

William P. Hopkins
October 23, 1981
Page Four


It is reasonably foreseeable that one or more of the units
which he manages will be subject to such delays and repairs,
in addition to the fees charged.

In addition, Mayor Seymour owns four residential rental
units himself. His investment exceeds $1,000. As to these
four units, the foreseeability of delays and repairs is
statistically much smaller1/ and probably fails to attain
the degree of foreseeability necessary to require disquali-
fication, standing alone.

As a homeowner, Mayor Seymour may also be affected by
the ordinance, in the event that he sells or rents out his
home. However, the prospect of a material financial effect
on his home is remote; therefore, foreseeability is lacking.

In addition, Mayor Seymour presumably sells residential
structures as a part of his real estate business. If the
ordinance is adopted, these sales would be subject to inspection
and possible requirement of repairs. This might have an
impact on his sales. It could conceivably lose him some
sales; on the other hand it might increase buyer confidence
and spur sales in Anaheim as opposed to the surrounding
area.

Given all of the foregoing possibilities for a material
financial effect on Mayor Seymour's financial interests, it
is our conclusion that he should refrain from participation
unless the impact on these interests is not distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.3/ Based upon the
Commission's guidance relative to the residential rental
property industry, laid down in its Ferraro Opinion,4/ we
conclude that the effect on Mayor Seymour is distinguishable
as to his rental management business and as to his ownership
of four residential rental units.5/

_________________________
3/ We have no information on the state of repair of
Mayor Seymour's units. More facts might lead us to a different
conclusion.
4/ Section 87103; 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.
5/ Opinion Requested by: John Ferraro, Opinion No.
73-009, 4 FPPC Opinions 62, at 65-67, Nov. 7, 1978. (Copy
previously provided.)
6/ However, we concluded that as to the four units
which he owns, we were unable to ascertain a foreseeable
material financial effect; if there were such an effect,
then disqualification would be required.

William P. Hopkins
October 23, 1981
Page Five


        Therefore, as to his rental property management interests,
he must disqualify himself because his interests are different
from those of the public generally and it is reasonably
foreseeable that adoption of the proposed ordinance may have
a material financial effect on his business.

     (b)  Councilmember Donald Roth:  Councilmember Roth's
situation is largely analogous to Mayor Seymour's, except
that Councilmember Roth does not own any residential rental
units.  He also is involved in managing residential rental
units; however, they are fewer in number, totaling only
seven.  Thus, the foreseeability of a material financial
effect on his financial interests is reduced; however, we
are advised by you that Mr. Roth feels that the ordinance
would have a financial effect on his management business.
In addition, Mr. Compton, his associate, has been outspoken
in opposition to the proposal on the grounds that it will
have an adverse financial effect on residential rental
properties.

     Assuming, as we must, that Councilmember Roth's and
Mr. Compton's opinions are accurate  and there is a material
financial effect within the meaning of the Political Reform
Act, then given all the foregoing, we conclude that it is
foreseeable that adoption of the ordinance in question will
have a material financial effect on Councilmember Roth's
financial interests.  For the reasons set forth in the discus-
sion regarding Mayor Seymour, above, the effect on Councilmember
Roth's interests is distinguishable from that on the public
generally.  Consequently, we conclude that Councilmember
Roth must refrain from any participation in the decision.

     (c)  Councilmember Overholt:  We do not have sufficient
facts regarding Councilmember Overholt's financial interests
to enable us to reach a conclusion.  He is an attorney and
has competent counsel.  Following our foregoing analysis as
well as that contained in the Ferraro Opinion and the Overstreet
Opinion,7/  you and Councilmember Overholt should be able to
determine whether or not he should refrain from participating.

_________________________
     7/  Opinion requested by:  Martin Overstreet, No.
80-010, 6 FPPC Opinions 12, March 2, 1981.  (Copy previously
provided.)

William P. Hopkins
October 23, 1981
Page Six


     (d)  Legally Required Participation: The Anaheim City
Council consists of five members. In the event that Councilmember
Overholt is required by the Political Reform Act to disqualify
himself, then, as a result of conflict of interest disqualifications,
there would not exist a quorum of the City Council to participate.
Consequently, the exception permitting participation, where
it is legally required, would apply. The application of
that rule is discussed in the Commission's Hudson Opinion.8/

CONCLUSION

     Mayor Seymour and Councilmember Roth must disqualify
themselves from participating in or in any way using their
official positions to influence the decision on the proposed
ordinance. Councilmember Overholt need not disqualify himself
unless he has represented clients who have paid him more
than $250 in the last 12 months and who have extensive resi-
dential property holdings (at least seven units) within the
City of Anaheim. Only if all three are required to disqualify
themselves will the rule of legally required participation
apply.

CAVEAT

     Because of the short time period in which this letter
had to be written, it has not been possible for you to supply
us with all of the detailed facts necessary for a more thorough
analysis. This letter is limited to those facts discussed.
If other facts come to light, then the advice might change
with the changing facts.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert B. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh

_____________________________
8/ Opinion requested by: Matthew L. Hudson, Opinion
No. 77-007, 4 FPPC Opinions 13, February 7, 1978. (Copy
previously provided.) See also Section 87101 and 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18701.

Oct 15 '81 AW 01
CITY OF ANAHEIM, CALIFORNIA
CIVIC CENTER, 200 South Anaheim Boulevard, Third Floor
Anaheim, California 92805

Office of
CITY ATTORNEY

Telephone:
714/999-5169

October 15, 1981


Robert E. Leidigh, Esq.
Counsel, Legal Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re:  Conflict of Interest - Occupancy Inspection Program

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

With reference to our telephone conversation of October 14, 1981, I would
appreciate your informal opinion on the following questions:

1. May City Council members who are real estate brokers engaged in the
management of rental residential property for a fee, vote on or participate
in the consideration of a proposed occupancy inspection ordinance for
residential rental property?

2. May City Council members who are real estate brokers who also own and
rent more than three residential rental units vote on or participate in
the consideration of a proposed occupancy inspection ordinance for residential
rental property?

3. May a City Council member who is also an attorney who handles legal work
for real estate firms and owners of residential rental property vote on or
participate in the consideration of a proposed occupancy inspection ordinance
for residential rental property?

4. May a City Council member who owns 20% of a real estate business that is
engaged in the management and ownership of residential rental property vote
on or participate in the consideration of a proposed occupancy inspection
ordinance for residential rental property?

(It is assumed that only residential rental property within the City
limits would be relevant to the above conflicts of interest questions).

Robert E. Leidigh, Esq.                    -2-                    October 15, 1981


The City of Pasadena has adopted an occupancy inspection program by ordinance
that provides as follows:

     "No person shall occupy, change the use of or sell, exchange,
     rent or otherwise permit any unit which is hereafter vacated
     by the occupant thereof to be reoccupied until a certificate
     of occupancy, periodic certificate of occupancy or temporary
     certificate of occupancy is issued by the administrator, as
     hereinafter provided."

The Pasadena ordinance applies to all residential structures except motels,
hotels, roominghouses and boardinghouses.

The City Council of the City of Anaheim is being petitioned by a neighborhood
organization to adopt an occupancy inspection ordinance similar to that of
Pasadena.

Two of the Anaheim City Council members are active real estate brokers who both
own and manage residential rental property. These City Council members have
asked the City Attorney for his opinion as to whether or not they have a con-
flict of interest within the purview of the Political Reform Act that would
prevent their making or participating in the making or using their official
positions to influence the proposed occupancy inspection ordinance.

The occupancy inspection ordinance requiring City inspections of residential
property may delay the rental of residential property until repairs, etc., are
made and thus result in a loss of management fees collected by the real estate
brokers. Also, the repairs ordered by City inspectors may involve expenditures
for property owned by the council members and may also delay rental of property
and loss of rental income.

The City Attorney has tentatively advised the real estate broker City Council
members that they should not vote or participate in the consideration of the
proposed occupancy inspection program based on the FPPC Opinion, dated
November 7, 1978, No. 78-009, In the Matter of Opinion requested by John Ferraro,
Councilman Los Angeles, involving a proposed Los Angeles City rent control
ordinance.

The above-cited Ferraro Opinion states on page four that "it is our belief
that the residential rental property business has sufficient attributes of an
industry to be characterized as such." Also, "Persons owning large numbers of
rental units or whose primary business actively revolves around ownership and
management of rental property clearly can be said to be part of the class that
make up the industry. They are persons who are more likely than not to be
most concerned with protecting and benefiting the common business interests of
the industry."

Basically, the same concerns expressed in the FPPC Ferraro Opinion on rent
control appear to be applicable when applied to an occupancy inspection
ordinance.

Robert E. Leidbgh, Esq.                    -3-                    October 15, 1981


If the owners of three or fewer rental units will not be affected by rent con-
trol decisions, then the owners of three or fewer rental units will not be
affected by an occupancy inspection ordinance.

The question that is presented is whether or not the City Council members have
a "financial interest in the decision -" if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally" on any real property of the official worth
$1000 or more?

Also, whether the $250 criteria is applicable as to the effect on the income
of a council member. The decision on the occupancy inspection program could
have a material effect on the income derived from the management of properties
by the real estate broker Council members. If income over $250 would be
affected for the Council members, would that be the threshold level that
require abstaining from voting on the occupancy inspection ordinance?

The attorney City Council member has contended that he does legal work for
real estate firms, owners of property, probate of estates, etc., and therefore,
he has a conflict of interest with respect to voting on the occupancy inspec-
tion ordinance. The question also arises as to what dollar amount or percentage
of the attorney's legal practice must be involved before it becomes a conflict
of interest for the attorney, assuming that the legal work makes him a part of
the real estate rental industry?

Please contact me if you have any questions concerning this matter.

Thank you for your assistance.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [illegible]
                                        WILLIAM P. HOPKINS
                                        CITY ATTORNEY

WPH/mbg
Enc.

sections.conclusion:
Mayor Seymour and Councilmember Roth must disqualify 
themselves from participating in or in any way using their 
official positions to influence the decision on the proposed 
ordinance. 
Councilmember Overholt need not disqualify himself 
unless he has represented clients who have paid him more 
than $250 in the last 12 months and who have extensive resi-
dential property holdings (at least seven units) within the 
City of Anaheim. 
Only if all three are required to disqualify 
themselves will the rule of legally required participation 
apply. 
CAVEAT 
Because of the short time period in which this letter 
had to be written, it has not been possible for you to supply 
us with all of the detailed facts necessary for a more thorough 
analysis. 
This letter is limited to those facts discussed. 
If other facts come to light, then the advice might change 
with the changing facts.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor John Seymour and Councilmembers Donald Roth and E. Llewellyn Overholt, Jr. must disqualify themselves from voting on a proposed Occupancy Inspection Program ordinance due to financial interests under Government Code Section 87100.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mayor Seymour must disqualify himself due to his substantial income from rental property management in Anaheim, and Councilmember Roth must also disqualify himself based on his property management business interests. Councilmember Overholt may participate unless he has substantial client relationships with extensive residential property holdings in the city.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Standard advice letter format with FACTS, ANALYSIS, and CONCLUSION sections. Contains detailed factual findings about three public officials and their financial interests. Document includes caveat about incomplete facts and references to prior FPPC opinions (Ferraro, Overstreet, Hudson).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Mayor John Seymour and Councilmembers Donald Roth and E. Llewellyn Overholt, Jr. must disqualify themselves from voting on a proposed Occupancy Inspection Program ordinance due to financial interests under Government Code Section 87100.

CONCLUSION: Mayor Seymour and Councilmember Roth must disqualify 
themselves from participating in or in any way using their 
official positions to influence the decision on the proposed 
ordinance. 
Councilmember Overholt need not disqualify himself 
unless he has represented clients who have paid him more 
than $250 in the last 12 months and who have extensive resi-
dential property holdings (at least seven units) within the 
City of Anaheim. 
Only if all three are required to disqualify 
themselves will the rule of legally required participation 
apply. 
CAVEAT 
Because of the short time period in which this letter 
had to be written, it has not been possible for you to supply 
us with all of the detailed facts necessary for a more thorough 
analysis. 
This letter is limited to those facts discussed. 
If other facts come to light, then the advice might change 
with the changing facts.

Hopkins City Attorney City of Anaheim Civic Center, Third Floor 200 South Anaheim Blvd. Anaheim, CA 92805 Re: Your Request for Written Advice Dear Mr. Hopkins: Thank you for your recent letter seeking the advice of the Fair Political Practices Commission. FACTS Based upon your letter of October 15, 1981, and our various telephone conversations, the available material facts are as follows: 1. The Decision The City Council of the City of Anaheim consists of four councilmembers and the mayor. At its meeting on Tuesday, October 27, 1981, the City Council will consider the adoption of a proposed ordinance establishing an "Occupancy Inspection Program," modeled on a program currently in effect in the City of Pasadena. You have provided me with a copy of the Pasadena Ordinance and the Pasadena program, the latter states, inter alia: The purpose of the Occupancy Inspection Program is to have residential structures inspected for safety and code compliance . . . each time a change of occupancy occurs. The ordinance, by its own terms applies to all residential structures, whether rented or owner occupied. It is triggered William P. Hopkins October 23, 1981 Page Two by a change in occupants or by a sale or other transfer of interest. No person shall occupy, change the use of or sell, exchange, rent, lease or otherwise permit any unit which is hereafter vacated by the occupant thereof to be reoccupied until a certificate of occupancy . . . is issued.... Before a certificate of occupancy may be issued an inspection must be performed by city officials to determine if the structure is habitable, in compliance with applicable health and safety codes. If the structure is found to be in compliance then the certificate is issued and is valid for 12 months or until the new occupants vacate, whichever comes later. If the structure is found not to be in compliance, then a notice of needed corrections is issued and these must be made prior to occupation, sale, etc. A fee of approximately $30 is charged by the City of Pasadena for its efforts. You have indicated that Anaheim may need to charge as much as $45, if such an ordinance is adopted. 2. The Public Officials (a) Mayor John Seymour: Mayor Seymour is a real estate broker. Mayor Seymour owns three single-family houses, and one condominium which he rents out. Mayor Seymour estimates the aggregate value of these properties to be $500,000; all are located in the City of Anaheim. In his business as a real estate broker, Mayor Seymour has earned approximately $70,000 in commissions in the last 12 months for managing numerous residential rental units in the City of Anaheim; including single-family dwellings, apartments
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to City of Anaheim's request regarding conflict of interest disqualifications for city council members voting on a proposed Occupancy Inspection Program ordinance. The Commission concludes Mayor Seymour and Councilmember Roth must disqualify themselves due to financial interests in rental property management, while Councilmember Overholt's situation requires further factual clarification.
